data.title: 古いノート
data.text: I found an old notebook from my university days at the bottom of a box last weekend. The handwriting was mine, but the thoughts felt like they belonged to someone else. Some ideas I'd underlined three times, clearly excited, were things I now take for granted. Other notes—worries about exams, a fight with a friend—meant everything then and mean nothing now. It made me wonder which of today's crises will seem just as small in ten years. Most of them, probably.

data.title: 修理の文化
data.text: In my grandfather's generation, fixing things was normal and buying new was a last resort. Shoes were resoled, shirts were patched, radios were opened and prodded until they worked again. Somewhere between then and now, repair became irrational. When a toaster costs twelve dollars, paying someone twenty to fix it makes no economic sense—but the economics hide what gets lost. When we stop repairing, we stop understanding how things work. When we stop understanding, we stop caring whether things are built to last. The cheapest product is often the most expensive, counted in what we've forgotten how to do.

data.title: 待合室の会話
data.text: In the waiting room at the dentist, an old woman sat next to me and asked if I was nervous. I said yes. She said she'd been coming here for forty years and it never gets easier. Then she showed me photos of her dog on her phone—a small brown dog named Biscuit. By the time the nurse called my name, I had forgotten I was nervous at all. Sometimes a stranger with a dog photo is the best medicine.

data.title: ゆっくりとした朝
data.text: I set my alarm fifteen minutes earlier than I need to, not to be productive, but to be slow. I make coffee without checking my phone. I look out the window at whatever the sky is doing. I sit with the cup until it's empty. The rest of my day is fast and full of other people's needs. These fifteen minutes are mine alone, and they cost almost nothing: just the discipline of not immediately reaching for the screen. It's the easiest improvement I've ever made to my life, and the one I protect most fiercely.

data.title: アルゴリズムの友達
data.text: My phone's algorithm knows me better than most of my friends. It knows what I read, when I sleep, what makes me click, and what I scroll past. It doesn't know me well—it knows me precisely, which is different. Knowing well means understanding why I do things; knowing precisely means predicting what I'll do next. The algorithm is excellent at the second and incapable of the first. It can serve me content I'll engage with, but it has no idea whether that engagement makes me a better or worse version of myself. That distinction is the one that matters, and it's the one no algorithm is designed to make.
